channel.title: Cadence 步频 — PT Research Signal
channel.description: Daily curated physical therapy & rehab research for clinicians. AI-scored signal from 50 sources.

item.title: Critically appraised paper: Mobile-based pulmonary rehabilitation improved exercise capacity as much as and health status more than centre-based pulmonary rehabilitation for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease [synopsis].
item.description: Mobile-based pulmonary rehabilitation improved exercise capacity as much as and health status more than center-based pulmonary rehabilitation for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
item.author: PubMed

item.title: The Psychometric Properties of the Trunk Impairment Scale Used After Stroke: A Systematic Review Using COSMIN Methodology.
item.description: A systematic review using COSMIN methodology evaluated the measurement properties of the Trunk Impairment Scale (TIS) in individuals with stroke, clarifying its psychometric properties.
item.author: PubMed

item.title: Two-Year Outcomes of the Proximal Hamstring Avulsion Rehabilitation Regimes: Longitudinal Versus Accelerated Protocol (PHARRLAP) Study: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
item.description: This prospective RCT (n=60) found that an accelerated rehabilitation pathway, compared to a conservative one, for proximal hamstring tendon repair showed comparable clinical outcomes, strength, and functional symmetry, with 2-year follow-up.
item.author: PubMed

item.title: Six-session non-operative exercise program yields sustained benefits for up to 18 months in end-stage knee osteoarthritis: a retrospective cohort study - Clinical Rheumatology
item.description: A six-session non-operative exercise program delivered over one year to end-stage knee OA patients awaiting TKR was associated with improvements in pain and patient-reported/performance outcomes, with some benefits persisting for up to 18 months.
item.author: Sports Medicine

item.description: This systematic review with longitudinal and multivariate meta-analysis details the recovery of quadriceps and hamstring muscle strength after ACL reconstruction.
item.author: BJSM

item.title: E-health StandingTall balance exercise for fall prevention in older people: results of a two year randomised controlled trial
item.description: Results of a two-year randomized controlled trial showed that StandingTall, a home-based e-health balance exercise program delivered through an app, provided an effective, self-managed fall prevention program for community-dwelling older people.
item.author: BJSM

item.title: Do blood flow restriction exercises offer additional benefits when compared to conventional exercises in musculoskeletal rehabilitation? A systematic review and meta-analysis.
item.description: A systematic review and meta-analysis of RCTs found that low-load exercise with blood flow restriction (LLE-BFR) offers additional benefits over low-load exercise (LLE) and high-load exercise (HLE) in improving muscle strength, disability, and pain intensity for individuals with musculoskeletal conditions.
item.author: PubMed

item.title: Frontline agreement in diagnosis and treatment for musculoskeletal dysfunctions. A prospective, randomized, controlled study.
item.description: This randomized controlled trial found high agreement in diagnosis, treatment, and discharge decisions between physiotherapists and resident physicians compared to senior physicians for musculoskeletal dysfunctions in an emergency department setting.
item.author: PubMed

item.title: Development of a Core Patient-Reported Outcome (Measures) Set for Pediatric Physical Therapy.
item.description: This study developed a core set of nine patient-reported outcomes (PROs) and corresponding measures for pediatric physical therapy, suitable for daily practice, research, and quality assessment, grouped into functioning, health, and well-being domains.
item.author: PubMed

item.title: Multi-Domain Benefits of Exergaming for Sarcopenia Management in Institutionalized Older Adults: Physical, Psychological, and Social Outcomes.
item.description: A 12-week pragmatic quasi-experimental study involving 72 institutionalized older adults with sarcopenia demonstrated multi-domain benefits (physical, psychological, social) from an exergaming intervention (Nintendo Switch Ring Fit Adventure).
item.author: PubMed

item.title: Sarcopenia Increases Adjacent Segment Degeneration Risk within 3 Years of Anterior Cervical Discectomy and Fusion.
item.description: An exploratory retrospective cohort study of 333 ACDF patients found that sarcopenia was associated with an increased risk of radiographic adjacent segment degeneration within 3 years post-surgery.
item.author: PubMed

item.title: Management and implementation of intermittent catheterisation in neurogenic lower urinary tract dysfunction: a clinical practice guideline in Germany – update 3.0 | International Urology and Nephrology | Springer Nature Link
item.description: This is an update to a clinical practice guideline in Germany for the management and implementation of intermittent catheterization in neurogenic lower urinary tract dysfunction.
item.author: Sports Medicine

item.title: Fusogenic therapy restored spinal cord function after complete transection - Spinal Surgery News
item.description: An international research team has restored motor, sensory, and pelvic functions after complete spinal cord transection in animal models using a combined fusogenic therapy and comprehensive rehabilitation protocol.
item.author: Spinal Surgery News

item.title: Improved Flexor Spasticity in Patients with Chronic Stroke Using a Dynamic Wrist-Hand Stretching Device: A Pilot Randomized Controlled Trial.
item.description: A pilot randomized controlled trial with 30 chronic stroke patients found that a novel dynamic wrist-hand stretching device significantly improved flexor spasticity and hand function compared to a control group.
item.author: PubMed

item.title: Efficacy and Modulating Factors of Repetitive Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation on Motor Function in Parkinson's Disease: A Meta-Analysis.
item.description: This meta-analysis evaluated the short-term and long-term therapeutic effects of repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) on motor function in Parkinson's disease (PD) and identified key modulating factors like stimulation site and frequency.
item.author: PubMed

item.title: Establishing weighted criteria for return to play after hamstring injury: a systematic review and methodological quality analysis.
item.description: This systematic review established weighted criteria for return to play after hamstring injury, highlighting the current lack of consensus and the need for standardized, multidimensional assessments to reduce recurrence rates.
item.author: PubMed

item.title: Shortwave diathermy for managing knee osteoarthritis: A systematic review with meta-analysis.
item.description: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 17 trials (N=1,372) found that shortwave diathermy (SWD) significantly improved pain (mean -2.13; 95% CI [-2.64, -1.62]; p < .01) and walking ability (mean -0.72; 95% CI [-1.08, -0.37]; p < .01) in patients with knee osteoarthritis.
item.author: PubMed

item.title: Preoperative physiotherapy and one-year patient-reported outcomes after primary total knee arthroplasty: a registry-based cohort study of 1,688 patients.
item.description: A registry-based cohort study of 1,688 patients found no significant difference in patient-reported outcomes and pain at 1 year post-primary total knee arthroplasty between those who received preoperative physiotherapy and those who did not.
item.author: PubMed

item.title: Short-term effects of therapeutic massage alone or combined with conventional physiotherapy in myofascial pain syndrome: A randomized controlled trial.
item.description: This RCT (N=60) compared therapeutic massage, conventional physiotherapy, and a combined approach for chronic upper trapezius myofascial pain syndrome, showing short-term effects on pain, pressure pain threshold, and functional disability.
item.author: PubMed

item.title: The WalkBack (walking and education) program for prevention of low back pain is acceptable to physiotherapists but relies on training, flexibility and system-level enablers to optimise implementation: a qualitative study.
item.description: A qualitative study exploring Australian physiotherapists' experiences with the WalkBack (walking and education) program for low back pain prevention found it acceptable but highlighted the need for training, flexibility, and system-level enablers for optimal implementation.
item.author: PubMed
